Glossary About
Phenomenology & the Brentano School

Jean Piaget

1896 – 1980

Cognitive development and the construction of mind through action.

Piaget’s philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as a genetic, anti-substantialist, action-based, normatively structured theory of consciousness.

In compressed form:

A mind is not a soul-substance hidden behind behavior. It is the progressively equilibrated organization of actions, operations, meanings, values, and norms by which a living subject constructs both an objective world and itself as a subject. Consciousness is not an inner light that passively reveals what is already there. It is a developmental reconstruction of action into meanings, concepts, implications, and values. The body belongs to the order of causality; consciousness belongs to the order of signification and implication. These two orders are not two substances that causally push each other around; they are two correlated aspects of conduct, related by structural isomorphism. Theology, in the early Piaget, expresses the same architecture at the level of value: God is not primarily an external supernatural cause but the immanent absolute of thought, truth, obligation, reciprocity, and love.

That reconstruction is supported by four major textual families: Piaget’s early religious-philosophical writings of the 1920s; his developmental accounts of the child’s world, dreams, animism, and egocentrism; his mature writings on consciousness, prise de conscience, and the cognitive unconscious; and his psychophysiological writings on causality, implication, affectivity, and value.

A useful warning: English-language receptions of Piaget often emphasize cognitive stages and neglect his early religious writings. The Hartford Institute notes that Piaget’s direct psychology-of-religion writings include his typology of immanence and transcendence and that these writings remained untranslated into English, which helps explain why they are less central in Anglophone accounts.

2. Why theology is not a distraction

Piaget’s early theological concerns are not detachable biographical curiosities. They are where several of his later philosophical distinctions first become visible.

The Fondation Jean Piaget records that Piaget’s adolescent interest in philosophy was stimulated by Auguste Sabatier’s philosophy of religion, encountered through his mother’s religious instruction. Sabatier’s project was to reconcile religious faith with science by grounding religious philosophy in the positive study of religious sentiment and the historical evolution of religions. A French OpenEdition chapter on Piaget likewise emphasizes his Protestant background, his early irritation with dogmatism and church institutions, his Christian-social concerns, his reading of Bergson, and his later movement from religious-philosophical questions into scientific genetic epistemology.

The important point is not that Piaget remained a conventional theologian. He did not. The important point is that his early theological problem is structurally continuous with his later philosophy of mind: how can value, norm, truth, obligation, and consciousness be real without being treated as supernatural causal forces or as subjective whims? French and Spanish-indexed scholarship has noticed this continuity more explicitly than much Anglophone reception. A Dialnet-indexed study, for example, frames the early Piaget in terms of continuity between theological/ethical categories and later biopsychopedagogical categories, especially the dialectic of immanence/transcendence and conflict/cooperation.

So the theological writings should be read as a prehistory of Piaget’s mature distinction between causality and implication. External transcendence corresponds to a divine causal power imposed from outside; immanence corresponds to the inner normativity of reason, value, and consciousness. This later becomes, in more technical language, the thesis that consciousness is not a causal energy but a system of significations and implications.

3. Piaget’s rejection of the “soul” as substance

Piaget’s mature psychology is deeply anti-substantialist. He does not deny consciousness; he denies that consciousness should be conceived as a spiritual substance or hidden entity called “soul.”

In his 1967 text “La conscience,” Piaget explicitly criticizes the older psychologies that attributed force, energy, or work to consciousness, as if consciousness were a quasi-material substance. He writes against the “soul as spiritual substance” model and praises the move in “psychologies without a soul” away from substantial faculties toward actions, relations, and transformations.

This is crucial. Piaget’s target is not inwardness as such. His target is the metaphysical reification of inwardness. “Soul,” in the disapproved sense, names a supposed entity that does causal work behind behavior. Piaget thinks this is a category mistake. It imports concepts from physics—force, energy, causation—into consciousness, where the appropriate concepts are meaning, implication, necessity, obligation, and value.

In a 1950 epistemological text, Piaget says that the problem is not to posit a “spiritual” substance or faculties of the soul. The genetic problem is to explain how operations, necessity, and understanding are constructed. The “mind” is therefore not a primitive given at the origin of development; it is a construction whose highest forms appear at the end of long developmental processes.

This yields the first extracted principle:

Piagetian philosophy of mind begins by replacing soul-substance with organized conduct. The subject is not an inner ghost. It is a living system of action whose structures become progressively coordinated, reflected, interiorized, symbolized, and normatively regulated.

4. “Mind” is not a thing but a developmental organization of operations

Piaget’s mature alternative to soul-substance is not behaviorism. It is an operational constructivism.

He treats action as primary. Mental life emerges from the coordination of action schemes. Infants do not begin with a clear “inner world” facing a clear “outer world.” Piaget’s developmental account says that at the beginning there is no stable differentiation between self and external world. Impressions are not yet attributed either to a personal consciousness or to independent objects; the infant’s world is an undifferentiated block that is neither properly internal nor properly external.

This is one of Piaget’s most radical claims. The self is not the starting point. The object is not the starting point either. Subject and object are co-constructed through the organization of action. The infant’s initial egocentrism is not narcissistic self-awareness. It is almost the opposite: the self is not yet known as a self. The world is centered on activity precisely because the subject is not yet conscious of itself as one object among others.

Piaget repeatedly describes development as a decentering movement. The child constructs objects, space, causality, and time in the first sensorimotor period; the child also gradually localizes inner life in the body and distinguishes mental events from external objects. This is a “Copernican” shift: the child moves from a world organized around undifferentiated action to a world in which the body is one object among others and the self is one subject among others.

The second extracted principle is therefore:

Mind is the developmental organization of action into reversible operations, not a pre-given container of representations. The subject becomes a subject by constructing objectivity; objectivity becomes objective through the decentering of the subject.

5. Consciousness: Piaget’s four-problem framework

Piaget’s mature account of consciousness is unusually precise. In “La conscience,” he distinguishes four problems:

First, there is global consciousness, the state of being awake or vigilant. Second, there are the laws of prise de conscience, the process by which a previously unthematized process becomes noticed, represented, or consciously regulated. Third, there are the specific contents of consciousness: meanings, significations, and their links. Fourth, there is the relation between consciousness and the body or brain.

The second and third problems are the most important for philosophy of mind. Piaget is not primarily asking, “Why is there something it is like to be conscious?” in the later analytic sense. He is asking: how does a living subject become aware of its own actions, operations, meanings, and norms? How do unconscious action structures become conscious conceptual structures? How do meanings relate to one another? How do conscious implications differ from physical causes?

This is why Piaget’s account can seem both close to and distant from contemporary philosophy of mind. It is not primarily a qualia theory. It is a theory of conceptual, operational, and normative consciousness. Its central concern is not raw feel but the formation of meaningful awareness.

6. Prise de conscience: consciousness is reconstruction, not illumination

Piaget explicitly rejects the idea that becoming conscious is a simple illumination. Consciousness does not merely shine a light on ready-made contents. Becoming conscious requires reconstruction.

In his 1974 work on prise de conscience, Piaget says that awareness of an action scheme transforms it into a concept. This transformation is not a passive copying of what action already contains; it requires active reconstruction. He also states that the epistemological importance of this process is immense: the interiorization of actions is one of the sources of logico-mathematical and causal structures.

Piaget’s famous law here is that awareness moves from the periphery toward the center. At first, the subject becomes aware of the goal of an action and its result: success, failure, obstacle, satisfaction, dissatisfaction. Only later does the subject become aware of the internal mechanism of the action: the means, coordinations, transformations, and reasons for success or failure.

This gives Piaget a non-Cartesian account of self-knowledge. The subject does not have immediate transparent access to itself. It often acts successfully before it understands how it acts. Action precedes conceptual self-knowledge. Success precedes understanding. Piaget’s later discussions of children and adults who can perform an action without being able to conceptualize it illustrate exactly this point: awareness is not a flashlight but a reconstruction that adds connections, relations, and generality.

The third extracted principle:

Consciousness is a developmental achievement of reflective reconstruction. To become conscious is not simply to inspect an inner object; it is to reorganize action into meanings, concepts, and implications.

7. The cognitive unconscious

Piaget’s unconscious is not primarily Freud’s dynamic unconscious of repressed wishes, although Piaget does not deny affective unconscious phenomena. In the cognitive domain, the unconscious consists largely of operative structures, action schemes, coordinations, and mechanisms that function without being explicitly conceptualized.

In “La conscience,” Piaget distinguishes processes that are consciously controlled from others that remain unconscious because they are automatic, habitual, or not yet objects of reflection. His focus is the cognitive unconscious: mechanisms of thought and action that can become conscious through development, conflict, or reconstruction.

This means that unconscious cognition is not irrational chaos. It may be highly organized. A child can act intelligently without knowing the principle of the action. An adult can coordinate complex gestures without being able to describe their mechanism. The unconscious is therefore often structurally richer than consciousness at a given moment. Consciousness catches up by reconstructing those structures conceptually.

Piaget even treats degrees of consciousness as degrees of integration. Some contents may be fleetingly conscious but not integrated into a conceptual or representational system. Sensorimotor activity may involve momentary awareness without stable conceptual consciousness.

The fourth extracted principle:

The unconscious, for Piaget, is not simply absence of consciousness; it is the unreflected operational infrastructure of consciousness. Consciousness develops by integrating, reconstructing, and normatively organizing this infrastructure.

8. Introspection is unreliable because the self is constructed

Piaget’s theory implies a strong critique of introspection. He does not deny that subjects report experiences. He denies that introspection gives immediate access to the formative mechanisms of thought.

Already in his early work on judgment and reasoning in children, Piaget argues that young children show little introspection and that awareness of thought develops gradually. He adopts Claparède’s idea that awareness often arises when automatic adaptation fails: we become conscious when there is difficulty, maladaptation, obstacle, or novelty. Introspection is therefore a second-degree awareness, not an original foundation.

In “La conscience,” Piaget criticizes philosophical introspection for importing preformed philosophical concepts into the description of mental life. He explicitly names Maine de Biran and Bergson in this context. The risk is that consciousness borrows metaphors from the physical world—force, tension, energy, work—and then mistakes those metaphors for immediate inner data.

This critique is important for philosophy of mind. Piaget is neither a Cartesian nor a simple empiricist. The subject is not transparent to itself, but neither is consciousness reducible to external behavior. Consciousness is real, but it must be studied genetically: by reconstructing how forms of awareness emerge from action.

The fifth extracted principle:

Self-knowledge is constructed and fallible. The subject must become conscious of itself through developmental reconstruction, social decentering, and conceptual coordination.

9. Child animism, dreams, and the “soul”

Piaget’s studies of children’s conceptions of the world are directly relevant to soul and consciousness. Children do not begin with a mature dualism between mind and matter. They gradually differentiate internal and external, subjective and objective, mental and physical.

In Piaget’s account, young children often conceive objects by analogy with themselves, especially as active, living, intentional, or conscious. This animism is not simply a doctrine that everything has a soul. It is a developmental confusion between activity, life, intention, knowledge, and movement. A cloud, for example, may be treated as knowing where it is going because it moves and brings rain or night.

Dreams are especially revealing. Piaget reports that children may treat dream images as coming from the person but existing externally during sleep, sometimes as if they go out into the room or onto the bed. When a child dreams of seeing himself, the result can be a doubling: the child in bed and the child in the dream as an image-like double.

This matters for the philosophy of mind because it shows that the notion of an inner mental image is not primitive. The child must learn to distinguish dream image from external object, thought from thing, intention from physical cause, moral rule from natural law, and self from world. Early “soul-like” notions are therefore not evidence of an innate metaphysics of spirit. They are transitional products of incomplete differentiation.

Piaget also describes children’s tendency to confuse natural laws with moral laws and physical determinism with obligation. This is another instance of the same developmental structure: causal, normative, and intentional orders are initially interwoven and only gradually differentiated.

The sixth extracted principle:

The concept of soul emerges developmentally from confusions and differentiations among image, body, action, intention, life, and consciousness. Mature thought does not begin by discovering an already-given soul; it differentiates the orders of physical causality, subjective meaning, social norm, and moral obligation.

10. Causality versus implication: Piaget’s decisive mind-body distinction

Piaget’s mature answer to the mind-body problem is built on a distinction between two orders: causality and implication.

In a 1963 text, Piaget says the problem should not be framed as “spirit and body” but as the relation between consciousness and physiological structures. He criticizes interactionism because making consciousness into a cause would require treating it as a force or energy, and force or energy belongs to the material-measurable order.

But he also rejects a crude epiphenomenalism. Consciousness is not useless vapor. It has its own mode of linkage: implication. For example, the truth that 2 + 2 = 4 implies that 4 − 2 = 2. One value can imply another value. Meanings can imply other meanings. These are not causal pushes. They are relations of signification, normativity, compatibility, necessity, and obligation.

In his 1954 discussion of consciousness and movement, Piaget explicitly says that the usual opposition between psychophysical parallelism and causal interaction is badly framed because both sides often import categories of substance, energy, and causality into consciousness. Consciousness should instead be understood as a system of implications between meanings. In its higher forms this includes logical necessities and moral obligations; in lower forms it includes signals and indices.

So Piaget’s mind-body position is neither dualist interactionism nor reductive materialism. Physiological processes are causal. Conscious processes are implicative. They correspond structurally, but they are not identical in conceptual vocabulary.

The seventh extracted principle:

The mental is not a second causal substance. It is the order of signification and implication correlated with bodily causality. Consciousness does not push the body as a ghostly force; it organizes meanings, necessities, values, and norms within conduct.

11. Isomorphism, not reduction

Piaget’s preferred solution is a form of psychophysiological parallelism, but this term can mislead. He does not mean that consciousness is an inert duplicate of brain processes. He means that there is an isomorphism between material causality and conscious implication.

The Fondation Jean Piaget’s presentation of Piaget’s work on consciousness summarizes the issue well: consciousness is what differentiates psychology from the other natural sciences; its proper notions include signification, comprehension, and implication; and Piaget criticizes the illegitimate transfer of force, energy, and work into the domain of consciousness.

Piaget also argues that psychological explanation requires both sides. Physiology explains causal mechanisms; psychology explains significations and implications. In a 1950 text he says that understanding and explanation, psychic implications and physiological causes, are irreducible but indissociable.

In 1966 he again describes psychophysiological parallelism as an isomorphism between material causality and the broad implication that links meanings in consciousness. Conscious translations can become rules, norms, and necessities.

This gives Piaget a subtle non-reductive position. Consciousness is not a separate substance; but its conceptual order cannot be replaced by physical causality without losing truth, normativity, obligation, and meaning. Conversely, consciousness cannot be treated as an independent causal power without mythologizing it as soul-force.

The eighth extracted principle:

Mind and body are two analytically distinct but developmentally inseparable orders within conduct: causal organization and implicative organization. The philosophical error is to force one vocabulary to do the work of the other.

12. Affectivity: energy, value, and the motor of mind

A complete Piagetian philosophy of mind must include affectivity. Piaget is often misread as a purely cognitive theorist, but he repeatedly insists that cognition and affectivity are inseparable in conduct.

In a 1947 text, Piaget says every conduct has both an energetic or affective aspect and a structural or cognitive aspect. Feelings regulate energies and values; intelligence provides structures. There is no reasoning without feeling and no affection without some form of understanding.

In his lectures on affectivity and intelligence, he says affectivity is the source of energy for the functioning of intelligence, but not the source of cognitive structures. Affectivity can accelerate or retard intellectual development, but it does not create or modify logical structures as such.

This is one of Piaget’s most important distinctions. Affectivity is not an irrational appendage. It is the energetic and axiological side of conduct: interest, desire, effort, satisfaction, frustration, valuation, will. Cognition is the structural side: schemes, classifications, operations, relations, reversibility. Neither side exists in isolation.

Piaget later restates this in interview form: interest and motivation are the motor of everything, but they do not determine that 2 + 2 equals 4. Affectivity can speed, slow, orient, or energize cognition; it does not alter the logical necessity of an operation.

The ninth extracted principle:

Mind is affectively energized structure. Cognition supplies form; affectivity supplies energy and value. Consciousness is therefore not merely representational but also axiological.

13. Will as affective operation

Piaget’s theory of will is especially relevant to philosophy of mind because it links affectivity, normativity, and operations.

In the 1947 text, Piaget compares will to affective operations on higher values. Will is not a mysterious faculty of the soul. It is an organization of values that can become stable, reversible, and conserved in a way analogous to logical operations.

This is an elegant anti-voluntarist and anti-reductionist theory. Will is not pure spontaneity. It is not a ghostly power. Nor is it reducible to mechanical impulse. It is the operational organization of affective values.

In broader Piagetian terms, willing involves coordinating immediate desires with more stable values, much as intelligence coordinates perceptual appearances with reversible operations. This is why moral development and cognitive development are deeply parallel. Autonomy is not the absence of structure; it is the internalization and reconstruction of structure.

The tenth extracted principle:

Will is not a soul-faculty but an operational organization of values. Freedom, in Piagetian terms, is not uncaused choice but increasingly autonomous regulation by reconstructed norms.

14. Moral consciousness, cooperation, and decentering

Piaget’s philosophy of mind is also social. Consciousness does not mature in isolation. Logical and moral objectivity develop through decentering, coordination of perspectives, and cooperation.

In his child-development account, Piaget describes the emergence of reflection and cooperation after early childhood. Logic is tied to the coordination of points of view, not merely to solitary maturation.

This social dimension also appears in the early religious writings. In the 1928 essay on immanence and transcendence, Piaget links transcendent religious authority with social constraint and heteronomy. The child or believer under external authority receives rules as sacred from outside. But in more differentiated and cooperative societies, the right of examination and reciprocal coordination become possible.

This is not merely sociology of religion. It is Piaget’s general theory of normativity. Heteronomy is norm imposed from outside; autonomy is norm reconstructed from within through reciprocity. In moral consciousness, as in cognition, development moves from egocentrism and external constraint toward cooperation and internalized necessity.

Piaget’s early theology therefore anticipates his mature social epistemology: objective reason is neither private ego nor imposed authority. It is impersonal normativity constructed through decentered coordination.

The eleventh extracted principle:

Rational consciousness is cooperative consciousness. Objectivity is achieved by decentering the ego and coordinating perspectives under norms that no single ego owns.

15. God as immanent norm, not external cause

The most philosophically important theological distinction in Piaget is between transcendent God as cause and immanent God as value or norm.

In the 1930 essay “Immanentisme et foi religieuse,” Piaget defines transcendence as exteriority and superiority, and immanence as inclusion within. He says a transcendent God is a God-cause: cause of the universe, miracles, and even intimate states of consciousness. Transcendence, in this sense, implies a specifically divine causality beyond natural laws.

By contrast, an immanent God is not a cause but a “reason” or value: not the source of events but the source or ground of truths and obligations. Piaget says values do not result from experience; rather, they found the possibility of experience. God, in this immanent sense, is the principle of intellectual and moral consciousness, the focal point of values required by consciousness.

This is not subjective psychologism. Piaget is careful to distinguish immanent God from the empirical ego. In the 1928 essay he writes that God is thought, not a being in the usual sense but the condition of existence; “thought” here is not psychological ego-thinking but the elaboration of values, ideal necessities, and norms.

This is the theological form of Piaget’s later implicative theory of consciousness. God is not a mental object or a cosmic person causing events from outside. God names the absolute normativity of truth, value, and obligation immanent to thought but transcendent to the individual ego.

The twelfth extracted principle:

Piaget’s mature anti-substantialism has a theological analogue: God is not an external causal substance but the immanent-transcendent normativity of consciousness.

16. Immanence, transcendence, and the empirical ego

Piaget’s immanentism can be misunderstood as reducing God to the human ego. He explicitly rejects that.

In the 1930 essay, he says there are three terms: transcendence, thought, and ego. To deny external transcendence is not to identify God with the ego. God may be immanent to thought while remaining transcendent relative to the empirical self.

This is a sophisticated position. Piaget wants to avoid both authoritarian transcendence and subjectivist relativism. The alternative is impersonal normativity: reason, truth, obligation, value. These are “in” consciousness, but not as private preferences. They are norms to which the ego must submit by reconstructing itself.

That same structure appears in mature Piagetian epistemology. Logical necessity is constructed, but not arbitrary. Moral autonomy is constructed, but not mere preference. Consciousness becomes more autonomous precisely by becoming less egocentric.

This gives a very important theological-philosophical result:

Immanence is not reduction to the ego. Immanence is the interior presence of norms that exceed the ego from within.

That sentence could almost serve as a bridge between Piaget’s theology and his psychology of consciousness.

17. Science and faith: explanation versus evaluation

Piaget’s 1923 “La psychologie et les valeurs religieuses” gives one of the clearest formulations of his early science-faith relation. He argues that science and religion conflict when both are treated as explanatory systems. The conflict is reduced if science is limited to experimental explanation and religion is understood not as explanation but as life, value, and affirmation of spiritual values.

He then applies this directly to psychology. A scientific psychology of religion must explain religious facts without appealing to transcendence; it must remain within biological and psychological interpretation. But this does not by itself abolish religious value. It changes what religious discourse can legitimately do.

Piaget’s conclusion is striking: science observes; faith evaluates. A science of values can explain how values function, but it does not itself evaluate as faith does. Faith in absolute value animates God-talk while avoiding ontological metaphysics.

Here again, the later mind-body distinction is already prefigured. Science belongs to explanation and causality. Faith belongs to valuation and normativity. The error is to make faith a rival causal science or to make science a destroyer of value.

The philosophical payoff is large:

Piaget’s world has no need for supernatural causal gaps, but it does have need for irreducible normativity. This is why his philosophy of mind cannot be reduced to naturalism in a crude sense. It is naturalistic about causes, but not eliminativist about meanings or values.

18. Love, reciprocity, and the highest value

Piaget’s theology of immanence is not purely intellectualist. It includes love and reciprocity.

In the 1928 essay, Piaget connects love of neighbor with reciprocity, calling it the affective aspect of a norm that is also central to intellectual life. Cooperation is practical reason. In the 1923 text, he also treats love as the supreme value in the sense that value is measured by fecundity: a value is higher when it engenders other values.

This helps correct a common caricature of Piaget as coldly cognitive. For Piaget, the highest form of mind is not computation. It is the integration of cognition, affectivity, value, reciprocity, and norm. Love is not opposed to reason; in his early theological vocabulary, it is the affective realization of reciprocity, just as logic is its intellectual realization.

The thirteenth extracted principle:

The mature mind is not merely logical; it is reciprocally and affectively normed. Love and reason are not identical, but they are parallel expressions of decentered reciprocity.

19. The “spiritual” reinterpreted

What, then, remains of the spiritual in Piaget?

Not soul-substance. Not supernatural causality. Not an external deity intervening in events. What remains is the order of value, obligation, truth, necessity, reciprocity, and self-transformation.

In the 1930 essay, Piaget says religious faith requires relation to the absolute, communion with the absolute, and transformation or consecration of life. He presents the struggle for the good and the true as collaboration with God.

But again, this God is not an external force. Piaget cites a Brunschvicg-like conception in which the religious idea emerges through the conditions of true judgment, not through a person in space and time.

A fair reconstruction is that Piaget spiritualizes normativity while de-supernaturalizing causality. The spiritual is not a second world of beings. It is the ideal pole of consciousness as it becomes capable of truth, value, reciprocity, obligation, and autonomy.

This makes Piaget neither conventionally secular nor conventionally theological. He is better described as an immanentist philosopher of normative consciousness.

20. Development as equilibration: the mind as self-correcting organization

Piaget’s deepest explanatory principle is equilibration. Conduct begins in disequilibrium and tends toward restored equilibrium. This applies cognitively and affectively. In one formulation, conduct begins with disequilibrium and ends in affective satisfaction or intellectual solution.

Assimilation and accommodation are the two poles of this process. The subject assimilates reality into existing schemes and accommodates those schemes to reality. Development is not passive reception but active reorganization.

This is essential for philosophy of mind because it prevents two opposite errors. The mind is not a mirror passively copying the world. But it is also not a sovereign subject imposing form arbitrarily. Mind develops through regulated exchanges between organism and environment.

In theological language, this corresponds to the move from egocentrism to immanent normativity. In cognitive language, it is the move from assimilation-dominated activity to equilibrated coordination. In moral language, it is the move from heteronomy and self-centeredness to reciprocity and autonomy.

The four vocabularies—biological, cognitive, moral, theological—are not identical, but in Piaget they share a deep developmental grammar.

21. Formal operations and the self-transcendence of thought

Piaget’s account of formal operations matters because it shows how consciousness can transcend the actual without invoking a supernatural faculty.

In his developmental sequence, conceptualization rises from material action to concrete operations and then to formal operations, where operations are performed on operations. Piaget describes reflective abstraction as a level at which thought can construct new relations beyond direct action and perception.

This is where mind becomes most “spiritual” in the reconstructed Piagetian sense. It can consider possibilities, necessities, implications, and systems. Consciousness is no longer tied only to actual action; it can operate on the possible.

Piaget’s 1950 text states that implications are incomplete in lower rhythms and regulations but become more complete in operational systems. At the level of operations, implication can even outstrip real causality because it can cover the possible, not merely the actual.

This is a crucial anti-reductionist point. Physical causality explains what happens. Formal thought can construct what could happen, what must follow if something is true, what would be contradictory, what is obligatory, what is possible. This is not supernatural, but it is irreducibly normative and modal.

The fourteenth extracted principle:

The mind transcends the actual through operations on the possible. This is Piaget’s naturalized account of rational transcendence.

22. A Piagetian map of the soul/mind/consciousness vocabulary

Piaget’s vocabulary can be organized as follows.

“Soul” names, in the rejected metaphysical sense, an imagined spiritual substance or faculty behind conduct. Piaget thinks this is a false substantialization of consciousness.

“Mind” is ambiguous. When it means a spiritual substance, Piaget rejects it. When it means the organized system of operations, meanings, values, and conscious implications emerging from conduct, it can be retained.

“Consciousness” has several senses: global wakefulness; momentary awareness; reflective awareness or prise de conscience; the domain of meanings and implications; and the subjective side of conduct correlated with physiological structures.

“Thought” does not mean private mental chatter. In the theological writings especially, thought can mean the impersonal normative order of truth, value, and ideal necessity.

“God,” in Piaget’s immanentist phase, does not mean a supernatural efficient cause. God means the absolute value or norm immanent to intellectual and moral consciousness but transcendent to the empirical ego.

“Spirit,” if used at all, should be understood not as substance but as normatively organized consciousness: truth, obligation, reciprocity, and value.

23. Extracted Piagetian philosophy of mind: systematic formulation

A comprehensive Piagetian philosophy of mind can be stated in the following theses.

First, the primitive datum is not the isolated mind but conduct: organism-environment exchange structured by action.

Second, subject and object are co-constructed. The self does not begin as a transparent Cartesian ego; it emerges through the same decentering processes that construct objectivity.

Third, consciousness is not a substance but a field of meanings, implications, values, and norms.

Fourth, becoming conscious is not inner illumination but conceptual reconstruction of action. Awareness moves from goals and results toward mechanisms and coordinations.

Fifth, the cognitive unconscious is the unreflected organization of action schemes and operations. It is often structurally competent before it is consciously understood.

Sixth, introspection is developmentally late and fallible. The subject must reconstruct itself; it does not immediately possess itself.

Seventh, physiological processes belong to material causality; conscious processes belong to implication and signification. The relation between them is isomorphism, not ghostly interaction.

Eighth, affectivity and cognition are inseparable aspects of conduct. Affectivity supplies energy, interest, and value; cognition supplies structure.

Ninth, will is an operational organization of values, not a metaphysical faculty of the soul.

Tenth, rationality is social and decentered. Logic and morality develop through coordination of perspectives and cooperation.

Eleventh, theology, when purified of external causal transcendence, expresses the absolute pole of normativity: God as immanent value, truth, obligation, and love.

Twelfth, mature consciousness is autonomous because it is governed by reconstructed norms, not because it is lawless.

Thirteenth, the highest mind is not merely adaptive but reflective: it can think possibilities, necessities, and ideals.

Fourteenth, a Piagetian philosophy of mind is therefore a genetic operational constructivism of consciousness and value.

24. Relation to major positions in philosophy of mind

Piaget is not a Cartesian dualist. He rejects the soul as spiritual substance and rejects causal interaction between consciousness and body.

He is not a reductive materialist. Meanings, implications, logical necessities, moral obligations, and values cannot be translated without remainder into physical causality.

He is not a behaviorist. Action is primary, but action is structured, meaningful, affectively energized, and progressively interiorized.

He is not an introspectionist phenomenologist. Consciousness is real, but introspection is late, partial, reconstructive, and often misleading.

He is not a simple functionalist in the computational sense. Operations matter, but they emerge from biological action, affective energy, social cooperation, and equilibration, not from formal symbol manipulation alone.

He is closest to a hybrid of genetic epistemology, biological constructivism, neo-Kantian normativity, developmental pragmatism, and non-reductive structuralism. But none of those labels is exact.

The best short label may be:

developmental implicative non-reductivism.

That is, mind develops; consciousness is implication; and the mental is not reducible to material causality even though it is inseparable from living bodily organization.

25. Theological reconstruction: Piaget as immanentist philosopher of consciousness

Piaget’s theological comments can be reconstructed into a coherent philosophy of religious consciousness.

Religion becomes problematic when it claims to explain events by divine causality. That puts it in competition with science. But religion remains meaningful when it concerns absolute value, transformation of life, communion with the ideal, and the normativity of truth and good.

Transcendent religion, in the problematic sense, mirrors heteronomous morality: authority comes from outside, imposes itself, and may inhibit autonomous examination. Immanent religion mirrors autonomy: norms are interior, but not private; they bind the subject as impersonal reason and value.

This is not atheism in a simple psychological sense, because Piaget still speaks of God, absolute value, communion, and collaboration with God. But it is not classical theism either, because God is no longer an external personal cause. The most accurate formulation is:

God is the immanent absolute of normative consciousness, transcendent to the ego but not external to thought.

This theological position clarifies the philosophy of mind. Consciousness, for Piaget, is not merely empirical awareness; at its highest it opens onto normativity that exceeds the empirical self. The self becomes free not by escaping norms but by internalizing and reconstructing them through reason, reciprocity, and love.

26. Main tensions and limits

The first major limit is that Piaget does not give a full theory of phenomenal consciousness in the contemporary “hard problem” sense. His theory is strongest for awareness, reflection, conceptualization, meaning, agency, normativity, and self-knowledge. It is less developed as a theory of raw qualitative experience.

The second limit is the ambiguity of psychophysiological isomorphism. Piaget avoids both reductionism and dualist interaction, but the exact ontological status of the isomorphism remains under-specified. Is it a methodological dual-aspect view, a structural realism, a biological monism with irreducible vocabularies, or a form of neutral monism? Piaget gives resources for each interpretation, but he does not settle the issue in the way later metaphysics would demand.

The third limit concerns affectivity. Piaget insists that affectivity is inseparable from cognition, but he often treats it as energy rather than structure. This allows a clean distinction between motivation and logical form, but it may understate the way affect can shape perception, salience, attention, and the very framing of problems.

The fourth limit concerns theology. Piaget’s immanent God may be philosophically elegant, but it will not satisfy classical theists who require divine personhood, creation, providence, or grace as more than normative ideals. Conversely, secular interpreters may suspect that the language of God is an unnecessary residue. The strongest reading is to treat Piaget’s theological language as a serious theory of absolute value, not as either orthodox doctrine or disposable metaphor.

The fifth limit concerns developmental universality. Piaget’s child studies opened a field, but many empirical details of stage theory and child animism have been debated. The philosophical architecture, however, does not depend on every empirical claim being preserved exactly as Piaget stated it.

27. Final synthesis

Piaget’s thought on soul, mind, and consciousness is best understood as a passage from substance to operation, from inner ghost to organized conduct, from external transcendence to immanent normativity, and from causal force to meaningful implication.

He rejects the soul as a hidden spiritual thing. He reconstructs mind as the developmental organization of action. He defines consciousness not as a passive inner theater but as the progressive conceptual and normative reconstruction of action into meanings. He treats affectivity as the energetic and axiological dimension without which intelligence would not move. He treats body and consciousness as inseparable but conceptually distinct: the body is causally organized; consciousness is implicatively organized. He treats theology as the highest early expression of the same problem: how can truth, value, and obligation be absolute without being imposed by an external causal power?

The resulting philosophy of mind is neither dualist nor reductive. It is a philosophy of living, acting, valuing, developing consciousness. Its most condensed formula would be:

Mind is the living construction of meanings and norms through action; consciousness is the reflective integration of those meanings; the body is their causal condition; affectivity is their energy; society is their decentering medium; and God, in Piaget’s immanentist theology, is the absolute value of truth, love, and obligation within thought, transcending the ego from within rather than ruling it from without.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
